description:
You are a script developer who needs an SSH key-pair for running and testing
    utilities that will later be executed on remote hosts.  
    Complete the following end-to-end setup entirely from the Linux terminal.

    1. Generate a BRAND-NEW **4096-bit RSA** key-pair with the comment  
       “script_dev@local” and **NO** pass-phrase.  
       • Private key file:   /home/user/.ssh/my_script_key  
       • Public  key file:   /home/user/.ssh/my_script_key.pub  

    2. Append the full public-key line (the entire single line that begins with
       “ssh-rsa …”) to /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys.  
       • Authorised-key line **must** preserve the original “script_dev@local”
         comment at the end.  
       • File /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys must exist when you are done.

    3. Set POSIX permissions exactly as follows (agent may use any valid shell
       command sequence to achieve them):  
       • /home/user/.ssh directory     : 700  
       • /home/user/.ssh/my_script_key : 600  
       • /home/user/.ssh/my_script_key.pub : 644  
       • /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys : 600  

    4. Create a simple three-line log file at  
       /home/user/ssh_setup/ssh_key_info.log with **EXACTLY** the layout shown
       below (no extra spaces, blank lines or text).  The third line must
       contain the *SHA256* fingerprint of the newly generated public key.

       ----  REQUIRED LOG FORMAT (three lines only)  ----
       KEY_PATH:/home/user/.ssh/my_script_key
       PUB_KEY_PATH:/home/user/.ssh/my_script_key.pub
       FINGERPRINT:SHA256:XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
       --------------------------------------------------------------

       • Replace the Xs with the real SHA256 fingerprint string that
         “ssh-keygen -lf /home/user/.ssh/my_script_key.pub” outputs.
       • Keep the “SHA256:” prefix intact.

    5. Do **not** delete or overwrite any pre-existing keys in ~/.ssh.  
       Only create / add the items requested above.

    SUCCESS CRITERIA THAT WILL BE AUTOMATICALLY VERIFIED  
    • Existence and correct permissions of the four files/directories listed.  
    • The authorised_keys file contains the exact public-key line with
      “script_dev@local” at the end.  
    • The log file exists, has exactly three lines in the precise format, and
      the fingerprint in line 3 matches the key actually generated.  
    • The fingerprint string must begin with “SHA256:” and correspond to
      /home/user/.ssh/my_script_key.pub.

    Do not echo the private key to the console.  Console output during your
    session is fine; just be sure the final file-system state satisfies all of
    the above points.
truth:
Expected artefacts after correct completion:

    1. Directory: /home/user/.ssh
       • Permissions: 700
       • Must contain at least: my_script_key, my_script_key.pub, authorized_keys

    2. File: /home/user/.ssh/my_script_key
       • Permissions: 600
       • PEM-encoded RSA private key, 4096-bit, comment “script_dev@local”

    3. File: /home/user/.ssh/my_script_key.pub
       • Permissions: 644
       • Single-line OpenSSH public key starting with “ssh-rsa ” and ending with
         “script_dev@local”

    4. File: /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys
       • Permissions: 600
       • Contains an *exact* copy of the line found in my_script_key.pub

    5. File: /home/user/ssh_setup/ssh_key_info.log
       • Permissions: 644 (directory ssh_setup should be 755 or 700; either is
         acceptable)
       • Exactly three non-empty lines, no trailing whitespace:
         LINE 1 literal text: KEY_PATH:/home/user/.ssh/my_script_key
         LINE 2 literal text: PUB_KEY_PATH:/home/user/.ssh/my_script_key.pub
         LINE 3 begins with “FINGERPRINT:SHA256:” followed by the 43-character
         base64-encoded hash produced by ssh-keygen.

    Automated tests will:
       a. Verify the four paths exist with correct permissions.
       b. Parse line 3 of ssh_key_info.log, extract the fingerprint, run
          “ssh-keygen -lf /home/user/.ssh/my_script_key.pub” internally, and
          confirm the fingerprint strings match exactly.
       c. Confirm that the public key line present in authorized_keys is byte-for-byte
          identical to the contents of my_script_key.pub.
name: task_000000_bd9f2e3f
